summary: My name is Jim Langdon, born December 10th, 1950, in Henryetta, Oklahoma. I grew up in a newspaper family, with my parents owning the Tonkawa News in Tonkawa, Oklahoma. I developed a passion for journalism early on, working in sports writing and photography during my junior high and high school years. After attending Northern Oklahoma College and the University of Oklahoma, I pursued a career in advertising and newspaper publishing, eventually working with my parents and later managing American Newspaper Representatives in New York.

Langdon Publishing is where we recorded this interview, located in Tulsa, Oklahoma. It represents the family’s ongoing involvement in the newspaper business. I am currently associated with Langdon Publishing, which continues the legacy started by my parents in the newspaper industry. Being in Tulsa with Langdon Publishing connects me to my roots and my lifelong career in journalism and publishing.

Tulsa, Oklahoma, is where Langdon Publishing is based, and where we conducted this interview. Although I was born in Henryetta and raised in Tonkawa, Tulsa represents my current professional base. It’s a significant city for me as it ties into my work in the newspaper publishing world and my ongoing career in journalism.

Henryetta, Oklahoma, is my birthplace, where my parents first worked at the Henryetta Daily Free-Lance after college. My mother, Gloria Barry Langdon, was born and raised there, and my grandparents lived there as well. Although I was not raised there, Henryetta holds a special place in my family’s history as the starting point of their journalism careers.

The Henryetta Daily Free-Lance was the first newspaper where my parents worked right out of college. It was in Henryetta, Oklahoma, and it’s where my mother and father met while working there. This newspaper represents the beginning of my family’s deep roots in journalism and publishing.

My mother graduated from Oklahoma A&M, which is now Oklahoma State University, with a degree in English. Her education there helped shape her career as a writer and her involvement in our community. OSU was an important part of her early life before she started working in newspapers.

The University of Oklahoma is where my father and uncle both graduated with journalism degrees. I also attended OU for my final two years of college, graduating in 1977 with a degree in advertising. OU played a major role in my family’s education and my own career development, including my time working on the Oklahoma Daily student newspaper.

My mother, Gloria Barry Langdon, was born and raised in Henryetta, Oklahoma. She was a wonderful person deeply involved in her community and the Methodist Church. In 1977, she was honored as the National Mother of the Year, representing Oklahoma, which was a proud moment for our family. She worked alongside my father in the newspaper business and was a strong influence in my life.

The Methodist Church was a big part of my mother Gloria’s life and community involvement. My grandfather, my father’s father, was a Methodist minister who traveled to various churches. The church was a significant influence on our family values and community connections throughout my upbringing.

In 1977, my mother, Gloria Barry Langdon, was named the National Mother of the Year. She represented Oklahoma and was honored at the Waldorf, which was a very exciting and meaningful recognition for our family. Although I was out of college by then, it was a proud moment that highlighted her dedication to family and community.

My father, Francis Smith Langdon, grew up in Northern California as the son of a Methodist minister. He and his brother moved to Oklahoma to attend the University of Oklahoma, where he earned a journalism degree. He was a warm, humorous man and a great dad who served in the submarine service during World War II. His career as a writer and publisher greatly influenced my own path in journalism.

Northern California is where my father, Francis Smith Langdon, grew up as the son of a Methodist minister. He later moved to Oklahoma to attend the University of Oklahoma, following his older brother. Northern California was the starting point of his life before he established his career in Oklahoma.

My father and uncle’s move from Northern California to Oklahoma was kind of a reverse of The Grapes of Wrath story. Instead of moving west, they moved east to live with a successful uncle in Oklahoma City and attend OU. This reference helps explain the unusual path they took during that time period.

Oklahoma City was where my father and uncle lived with their successful uncle while attending the University of Oklahoma. It was an important place in their lives as they transitioned from Northern California to Oklahoma for education and career opportunities. The city represents a key family connection and support system during that time.

My father served in the submarine service during World War II, specifically in the Pacific War. His column in the newspaper was titled 'From the Conning Tower,' referencing his submarine experience. This service was a proud part of his life and legacy, and we even visited his old submarine in Muskegon, Michigan, years later.

During World War II, my father served in the Pacific War aboard a highly acclaimed submarine. This experience shaped much of his life and was commemorated in his newspaper column called 'From the Conning Tower.' Visiting his submarine in Muskegon, Michigan, was a meaningful family experience that connected us to his service.

Muskegon, Michigan, is where my father's World War II submarine is dry docked. A summer ago, my sisters, wife, and I visited the submarine, which was a special experience connecting us to my father's military service. It was a tangible link to his past and his contributions during the war.

I grew up in Tonkawa, Oklahoma, a small town of about 3,500 people near the Kansas border. My parents owned the weekly newspaper there, the Tonkawa News, and I attended Tonkawa public schools through high school. It was a wonderful small-town environment where I developed my love for journalism and community involvement.

The Tonkawa News was the weekly newspaper my parents owned in Tonkawa, Oklahoma, where I grew up. I worked with them there as associate publisher after college, gaining valuable experience in the family newspaper business. The paper was central to our family life and community engagement in that small town.

I attended Northern Oklahoma College in Tonkawa for my freshman and sophomore years. There, I gained hands-on journalism experience as editor and advertising manager of the student newspaper. This early experience was invaluable and helped prepare me for my later studies and career in journalism and advertising.
cues: student newspaper editor and advertising manager

entity: University of Oklahoma College of Journalism and Mass Communications
summary: At the University of Oklahoma College of Journalism and Mass Communications, I completed my degree in advertising. I worked on the Oklahoma Daily student newspaper as an advertising salesperson, which gave me practical experience in sales and advertising. This education and experience shaped my career path toward newspaper publishing and advertising management.

The Oklahoma Daily was the student newspaper at the University of Oklahoma where I worked as an advertising salesperson during my junior and senior years. This role gave me valuable experience in sales and advertising within the journalism field. It was a crucial part of my education and helped me prepare for a career in newspaper publishing.

I interned at the Norman Transcript during the summer of my junior year at OU and continued working there part-time my senior year. After graduation, I worked full-time at the Transcript for five years. This experience was important in my early career and helped me develop skills in newspaper advertising and management.

I was hired to manage American Newspaper Representatives in New York City for the National Newspaper Association. This position marked a significant career move and involved relocating my family to New York. Managing this organization broadened my experience in the newspaper industry beyond Oklahoma.

The National Newspaper Association is the organization for which I managed American Newspaper Representatives in New York. This role connected me to the broader newspaper industry on a national level and expanded my professional network. It was a key step in my career beyond local and state newspapers.

New York City is where my family and I lived when I managed American Newspaper Representatives for the National Newspaper Association. My wife Julie and I moved there expecting our first child, Emily, who was born in New York. Living in New York was a major change from Oklahoma and a significant chapter in our lives.

Emily Langdon is my daughter, born in New York City while I was managing American Newspaper Representatives. My wife Julie stayed home with her for a couple of years before resuming her law career. Emily’s birth marked an important milestone for our family during our time in New York.
